coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor for cadmium ion detection
coredata.dc:creator: Abinaya, S.
Girija, S.
Baby, Anjana
Banu, A. Aseema
Jose, Sujin P.
coredata.dc:description:
Cadmium (Cd2+) ions pose significant risks due to their toxic effects even at low concentrations. By integrating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with α-Fe2O3 and polyaniline (PANI), we have engineered a nanocomposite that demonstrates a remarkably high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Cd2+ ions. The NCQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite was prepared by using hydrothermal and in-situ polymerization methods. The characterization techniques for an eco-friendly polymer nanocomposite confirm the interactions between N-CQDs, α-Fe2O3, and PANI. X-ray diffraction analysis shows the nanocomposite possesses a crystal size of 24
±
0.03 nm. Field-emission scanning electron microscope and high-resolution transmission electron microscope images showed that the spherical N-CQDs are enclosed by irregular α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, which are dispersed on the PANI sheets. Further, the particle size distribution analysis indicates an average size of 5.8 nm for the N-CQDs. The electrochemical sensing result suggests that the nanocomposite is effective in sensing the Cd2+ ions with a detection limit of 750 nm.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor for cadmium ion detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Cadmium (Cd2+) ions pose significant risks due to their toxic effects even at low concentrations. By integrating nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with α-Fe2O3 and polyaniline (PANI), we have engineered a nanocomposite that demonstrates a remarkably high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Cd2+ ions. The NCQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite was prepared by using hydrothermal and in-situ polymerization methods. The characterization techniques for an eco-friendly polymer nanocomposite confirm the interactions between N-CQDs, α-Fe2O3, and PANI. X-ray diffraction analysis shows the nanocomposite possesses a crystal size of 24
±
0.03 nm. Field-emission scanning electron microscope and high-resolution transmission electron microscope images showed that the spherical N-CQDs are enclosed by irregular α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, which are dispersed on the PANI sheets. Further, the particle size distribution analysis indicates an average size of 5.8 nm for the N-CQDs. The electrochemical sensing result suggests that the nanocomposite is effective in sensing the Cd2+ ions with a detection limit of 750 nm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for fresh, clean water worldwide underscores the urgent need for precise water quality monitoring. Emerging sources of pollution pose significant threats to water quality with heavy metal ion contamination ranking among the most pressing environmental challenges. Heavy metals are non-biodegradable and highly toxic, leading to harmful effects on living organisms. Predominantly arising from human activities such as emissions from coal-burning plants, smelters, and industrial smokestacks these pollutants persist in the environment for decades even at trace levels and can accumulate through the food chain ultimately impacting human health and the ecosystem. Even in trace amounts, heavy metals are toxic and can cause severe environmental and health risks. When these metals enter the human body through ingestion, inhalation, or skin contact they can induce nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, or other adverse reactions making the need for effective monitoring and mitigation essential. Some of the industrial processes that cause the consumption of heavy metal ions are electrolysis, electroplating, pesticide manufacturing, paint formulation, printing, dyeing, and textiles inevitably lead to the generation of wastewater containing high concentrations of heavy metal ions. Once a heavy metal ion enters a cell, it disrupts the biochemical processes by bonding with protein thiol groups, making it hazardous. Recognizing these risks, major organizations including the World Health Organization, Food and Agriculture Organization, Centers for Disease Control, and the International Agency for Research on Cancer are actively studying the heavy metal ion toxicity. Consequently, developing rapid and accurate methods for detecting low concentrations of heavy metal ions in the environmental, food, medical, and biological samples is critically important [1–3]. This industrial wastewater also poses significant threats to the ecological environment. In light of these pressing challenges, there is an urgent need to identify suitable materials capable of rapidly detecting and effectively removing heavy metals from wastewater [4]. Achieving both high sensitivity in the detection and efficient removal of heavy metal ions is crucial for mitigating the environmental impact of industrial activities [5,6]. Many analytical techniques are commonly used to detect heavy metal ions. These techniques include electrochemical methods such as amperometry, voltammetry, and potentiometry; spectroscopic methods like atomic absorption spectroscopy and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry; and optical methods using indicator dyes and ionophores [7,8]. Of these, electrochemical methods are frequently chosen because of their affordability, dependability, and speedy turnaround for analysis. They might however be less sensitive than other approaches [9].

Cadmium (Cd), a Group II B element and known hazardous heavy metal is released into the environment through industrial operations such as mining, smelting, battery production, and electroplating. It was identified as the seventh most hazardous substance by the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry priority list for 2022. It is bioaccumulative, persistent, and toxic with human activities like burning fossil fuels and industrial emissions that all have a major role in the occurrence of Cd in the environment [10,11]. Exposure to Cd is also linked to reproductive toxicity in both animals and humans affecting both male and female reproductive systems. Cd has been a source of detrimental effects on the morphology, physiology, and biochemical functions of male reproductive organs. In female reproductive systems, experimental animal studies have demonstrated that Cd can induce temporary sterility. Long-term exposure to low levels of Cd has also been found to adversely impact the health of subsequent generations with certain harmful effects showing gender specific differences in offspring [12]. It is also associated with a range of serious health conditions including obstructive airway diseases, emphysema, end-stage renal failure, and complications such as diabetes and renal dysfunction. It can also lead to deregulated blood pressure, bone disorders, and immune system suppression. Studies have reported Cd concentrations in dust ranging from 0.25 to 14.5 mg/kg. Additionally, Cd has been detected in significant amounts in the liver (60–62 µg/g), pancreas (40–50 µg/g), and thyroid (35–45 µg/g) of individuals residing in contaminated areas. Hence, the necessity to detect Cd ions stems from various factors primarily due to the potential environmental and health hazards. Thus, it is essential to create straightforward, efficient, and sensitive techniques for detecting and monitoring cadmium ions using diverse nanocomposite materials. These challenges motivated us to focus on developing electrochemical sensors specifically for cadmium ion detection.

Emerging nanomaterials such as metal oxides, carbon, and polymer-based nanocomposites, have shown great potential in water treatment applications [13]. It is worth noting that the numerous chemically modified electrodes have been explored for the detection of Cd2+ ion across various sample matrices. These include carbon paste electrodes modified with polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer-functionalized iron oxide nanoparticles, glassy carbon electrodes modified with graphene/gold nanoparticles integrated with modified L-cysteine nanomaterial, Fe3O4/Bi2O3/g-C3N4 nanocomposites, engineered multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), bismuth/MWCNT–emeraldine base polyaniline–Nafion composites, BiOCl/MWNTs, and bismuth nanoparticles [14]. Further, Lee et al. developed an electrochemical sensor for the detection of Cd2+ ion using Fe2O3/graphene nanocomposite electrode combined with in-situ deposited bismuth [15]. The enhanced performance of these electrode materials has demonstrated promising capabilities for the sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions primarily due to their unique structural, conductive, and adsorptive properties. They are increasingly being used to effectively remove a wide range of contaminants from water sources [16]. However, Fe2O3, carbon, and polymer-based nanomaterials are explored for the effective detection of cadmium ion (Cd2+). Their combined synergistic effect of N-CQDs with α-Fe2O3 and PANI in sensor application require further investigation and not yet explored.

Among several nanomaterials, iron oxide (α-Fe2O3) nanoparticles shine with their combination of cost-effectiveness, greater performance, high surface-to-volume ratio, and eco-conscious nature showcasing immense promise for heavy metal ion sensing applications due to their outstanding chemical properties and environmental suitability. Its high surface area and redox active sites helps in adsorption and reduction of Cd2+ ion [17]. Over the past decade, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as highly effective analytical sensors owing to their exceptional water solubility, biocompatibility, optical properties which enhance the photoluminescence, and non-toxic nature. It possess various functional groups such as hydroxl, carboxyl, and amino groups that contribute to water solubility and easy functionalization [18–24]. They are zero dimensional (0D) nanostructures where their diameter ranging from 2 to 20 nm [25–27]. Moreover, they show remarkable quantum confinement and tunnelling effects. This positioning makes it potential for diverse applications in electrochemical sensing and detection technologies [28]. Electronically conducting polymers have attracted a lot of attention because of their remarkable electrochemical and physical properties. Among them, polyaniline (PANI) has attracted attention due to its ease in synthesis, exceptional doping/ dedoping, stability, and low-cost [29,30]. This conductive polymer matrix undergoes reversible redox reactions due to its excellent electron transport properties. Current research has shown that doped carbon nanomaterials filled with heteroatoms can effectively alter their intrinsic qualities including electrical behavior and natural traits. These achievements highlight the possibility of novel innovations in electrochemical sensing applications using CQDs based nanocomposites [31–33]. The integration of these components i.e., N-CQDs with α-Fe2O3 and PANI facilitate the development of electrochemical sensor of Cd2+ ions. This sensor employs a blend of nanostructured nanocomposites, featuring PANI coated α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles along with N-CQDs. This interaction can enhance the sensing performance due to the synergistic effect of the individual components and by overcoming the limitations of individual materials like low conductivity of α-Fe2O3, low selectivity due to excessive surface functionalization of N-CQDs, and limited long-term stability of PANI. N-CQDs enhance the composites reactivity, selective binding, and interaction with target ions due to the abundance of functional groups. These functional groups also facilitate strong interactions with α-Fe2O3 and PANI, enabling effective detection of toxic metal ions. The trigonal structure of α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles contributes to the composite’s stability and broadens its sensing capabilities. Meanwhile, PANI adds flexibility and conductivity, promoting efficient electrons and enhancing the overall functionality of the nanocomposite. Hence, this combination leverages the individual advantage of each nanomaterial results in a hybrid nanocomposite with the enhanced performance of sensitive and selective detection.

This study involves the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@α-Fe2O3/polyaniline (N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI) nanocomposite using in-situ and hydrothermal techniques. This synergistic combination improves the material’s chemical stability, sensitivity, and suitability for environmental applications such as detecting and removing cadmium from contaminated environments. Through, an in-depth analysis, we thoroughly explored the electrochemical characteristics of the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite. This innovative approach to nanocomposite preparation holds promise for achieving a significantly lowered detection limit i.e. limit of detection (LOD) in the electrochemical sensor intended for heavy metal ion detection. The ternary N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite is used for the detection of Cd2+ ions through electrochemical sensing. We anticipate that the utilization of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite based electrochemical sensor is expected for the effective management and reduction of Cd2+ ion contamination to safeguard environmental and public health.

Glycine (NH2-CH2-COOH), urea (CO(NH2)2), aniline (C6H5NH2), ethanol (C2H5OH), distilled water, ice bath, ammonium peroxydisulfate (APS) (NH4)2S2O8, pyrrole (C4H4NCH3), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), 0.22 µm syringe filter, and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from M/s Sigma Aldrich, USA and used without further purification.

The N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite synthesis was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The nanocomposite was synthesized using pre-prepared N-CQDs, and the precusors FeCl3.6H2O, urea, and an aniline monomer. Initially, N-CQDs were synthesized by grinding 0.15 g of glycine (NH2–CH2–COOH) and 0.72 g of urea (CO(NH2)2) in a mortar and pestle, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 2 h. The resulting product was washed through centrifugation with distilled water to remove unreacted residues, then filtered using a 0.2 µm syringe filter to obtain approximately 20 mL of N-CQDs. Next, 15 mL of the N-CQDs from the obtained solution was combined with 0.58 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.2 g of urea in ethanol and maintained at 40 °C. This mixture was then hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 24 h to obtain N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3, which was subsequently washed and vacuum dried at 70 °C. Finally, 2 g of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 was mixed with 0.465 g of aniline dissolved in distilled water and stirred in an ice bath. A solution of 2 g APS in 0.5 M HCl was then added dropwise to this mixture and allowed to react for 24 h, yielding the ternary N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite.

For electrode fabrication, 8:2 ratio of ethanol and water used as a solvent to disperse 1 mg of N-CQDs, N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3, and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI respectively. The solutions were sonicated for 60 min in an ultrasonic bath. By polishing with alumina slurry and drying it in an oven, now glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was prepared for coating. Then, 8 μl of the material was drop casted on the surface of the GCE and dried at ambient temperature. In later electrochemical investigations of the prepared materials were done by using 0.1 M KCl in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as standard electrolyte. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) electrolytes were used in cyclic voltammetry (CV), pH experiment, repeatability tests, stability, reproducibility analysis, and square wave voltammetry (SWV) analysis. All the experimental procedures were carried out at a scan rate set to a 50 mVs-1, but in the scan rate study, we varied the scan rate from 10 mVs-1 to 1 Vs-1.

The research employed advanced analytical techniques to thoroughly characterize the synthesized materials. Crystal structures and phases were identified using a Panalytical X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu-Kα radiation. Crystallite size calculations were made using the Modified Williamson-Hall method. For the identification of functional groups fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded using a JASCO FT/IR-4600. Structural and morphological properties were examined through field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX), providing elemental composition details of Fe, C, N, and O. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured with the FEI Tecnai G220 S-TWIN TEM, with particle sizes analyzed using ImageJ software. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using ESCALAB 250xi BASE SYSTEM WITH UPS AND XPS IMAGE MAPPING (Thermo Scientific). Electrochemical measurements were performed with a CHI 680 electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments, Austin, USA). All electrochemical experiments including CV, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and SWV were conducted using a three-electrode setup. This setup consisted of a GCE as the working electrode, a saturated silver electrode (Ag/AgCl) as the reference electrode (in 1 M KCl solution), and a platinum wire serving as the counter electrode. CV and SWV studies were carried out in PBS with a pH of 5.0, within a potential range of −1 to +0.5 V. EIS measurements were conducted under similar conditions using a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as an electrochemical probe covering a frequency range from 0.1 kHz to 100 kHz.

The XRD pattern of N-CQDs in Fig. 1 a exhibits a broad peak around 23° corresponding to the (h k l) plane of (002) that indicates the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms [29,34]. The XRD pattern exhibits the characteristic reflections of the R-centered trigonal phase of the α-Fe2O3 with a space group of R-3c. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1a, which validates the trigonal phase of α-Fe2O3 in N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 ensures the material’s crystallinity and stability. This can strengthen the high conductivity, efficient electron transfer, and enhanced surface area for the analyte binding. It also shows that the (h k l) planes (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214), and (300) corresponding to 2θ° values of 24.1°, 33.1°, 35.7°, 40.9°, 49.5°, 54.0°, 62.5°, and 64.1° are well matched with JCPDS card No. 33–0664 and are compared with the previous literature [35–37]. The absence of new peaks suggests that no further modifications have occurred and a finding that is corroborated by a comparison with JCPDS data. The addition of the amorphous nature of PANI can enhance the electrochemical interactions which provides abundant active sites for the adsorption of analytes. Hence, the integrated N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite improves the sensor’s sensitivity, selectivity, and long-term stability making it a reliable platform for electrochemical sensing applications. Further, the crystallite size and lattice strain were calculated from Modified Williamson-Hall (MWH) plot which was depicted in Fig. 1b–c. As this method accounts for both size and strain contribution to peak broadening, it is considered to be more reliable than traditional debye scherer equation. The MWH plot was constructed based on the measured full width at half maximum (FWHM- denoted as β) values from Braggs peaks such as (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214), and (300). The plot is constructed by plotting 4sinθ versus β cos2θ, where θ is calculated from the Braggs angle (2θ). The slope of the resulting linear fit provides the lattice strain (ε), and the y-intercept yields the crystallite size (D). This method of analysis offers a comprehensive analysis of the materials microstructure which makes it an effective tool in XRD studies. This approach represents the crystallite size and strain of the prepared nanocomposite which are found to be 26
±
0.88 nm and 1.1 x 10−3 for N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 and 24
±
0.03 nm and 8.375 x 10−4 for N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI. Smaller grain size results in higher surface to volume ratio and faster electron transfer due to smaller diffusion paths that can enhance the interaction of analytes and the conductivity of the electrode material. The smaller grain size also enhances the diffusive capacity of the electrode material and can increase the density of grain boundaries which acts as an active site for the adsorption of analytes. It is also found that the increase in grain size can influence the decrease in electrochemical performance. The reduced strain induces smoother pathways for ion migration enhancing charge transfer and sensor response. It also results in long term durability in electrochemical sensing applications due to its relaxed lattice structure [38].

In Fig. 2 a, the Fourier transform infrared spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 show the presence of a peak at 820 cm−1 indicating the carbon–carbon double bonds of CC bending vibration. The oxygen containing functional groups occurred at 1085 cm−1 and 1256 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C, signifying oxygen-based chemical bonds, and the symmetric stretching vibration of aromatic amine C–N. Additionally, the presence of nitrogen occurs at 1385 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N stretching vibration of N-CQDs [29,39–41]. Further, the band at 1660 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CC bonds, further indicating the presence of carbon–carbon double bonds. The successful integration of α-Fe2O3 into the N-CQDs matrix was confirmed by the appearance of the Fe–O stretching vibration at 495 cm−1 and CC stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1. In Fig. 2b, the band observed around 400 to 500 cm−1 is indicative of the Fe-O bond, confirming the presence of metal–oxygen interaction within the composite structure. The addition of N-CQDs can be inferred by the band at 1033 cm−1 that corresponds to C-N stretching vibration [42]. The presence of N-CQDs and PANI coatings is confirmed by the following functional groups. It is found that the band at 1291 cm−1 is attributed to the C-N stretching vibration [43]. Furthermore, the benzenoid form is observed at 1480 cm−1, which indicates the oxidation state of the emeraldine salt of PANI [44–46]. In addition, the band at 1560 cm−1 confirms the presence of CC stretching of the quinoid ring [44]. The stretching vibrations observed in the range of 3400 to 3500 cm−1 further indicate the presence of O–H/N–H groups [47].

The FESEM image (Fig. 3 a) of binary nanocomposite depicts that the as prepared α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles and N-CQDs are agglomerated with an overall spherical morphology. This spherical morphology can provide efficient electron transport that improves the conductivity of the sensing material [48,49]. After the inclusion of PANI the surface morphology of the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite shows a series of flakes with lumps formation (Fig. 3d) [50]. The addition of PANI results in enhanced surface area and provides more active sites for the adsorption of analytes. However, the irregular shapes of the individual nanocomposite could not be decided accurately due to the agglomeration of nanoparticles. The EDAX spectra (Fig. 3b and e) and the corresponding elemental compositions (Fig. 3c and f) of the synthesized nanocomposite were observed with EDX spectral analysis. It exhibits the purity of the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite with the atomic and weight percentage of the elements carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and iron (Fe) with no other extra impurities.

Further, the morphological feature was explored through HRTEM analysis. The above images show the spherical N-CQDs are enclosed with irregular α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles (Fig. 4 a–c) [51,52]. Additionally, the average particle size of binary composite, N-CQDs@ α-Fe2O3 was found to be 158 nm (Fig. 4d). Fig. 4e–g shows the HRTEM images of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite. The high magnification HRTEM images which confirm the formation of N-CQDs and PANI sheets in N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite. It offers a larger surface area compared to bulk materials, which enables improved electron transfer and enhanced electrochemical activity. At Fig. 4g, the inset image depicts that the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles are well dispersed on the PANI nanosheets which provide good chemical stability [53]. This dispersion is crucial for preventing α-Fe2O3 and N-CQDs from degradation in harsh sensing environments. Due to the dispersion of the particles, the particle size of the ternary composite was not feasible. However, in N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite the particle size of N-CQDs was found to be 5.8 nm (Fig. 4h). Incorporating PANI into N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 enhances sensing performance by amplifying surface area, conductivity, chemical robustness, and collaborative interactions among the constituents. The synergistic effect of the well distributed ternary nanocomposite and the small particle size of N-CQDs are predictable to result in superior electrochemical sensing performance for metal ion detection.

To investigate the surface chemical composition and the active sites responsible for Cd2+ ion detection, XPS was performed on both N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposites (Fig. 5 a–j). The full survey spectra (Fig. 5a and f) confirm the presence of Fe, O, C, and N elements in both samples, consistent with the incorporation of N-CQDs and PANI on the α-Fe2O3 framework. The high-resolution Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 5b and g) exhibit characteristic peaks at ∼ 710.9 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and ∼ 724.4 eV (Fe 2p1/2) along with a satellite peak around 718.6 eV indicating the presence of Fe3+ in α-Fe2O3. No significant changes were observed in the Fe oxidation state upon PANI incorporation, suggesting that the metal ion detection mechanism is mainly governed by surface functionalities rather than redox transitions of Fe [54–56]. The C 1 s spectrum of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 5c) shows peaks corresponding to C–C/CC (∼284.7 eV), C–O/C–N (∼286.1 eV), and CO (∼288.3 eV), indicating the presence of oxygenated and nitrogenous functional groups derived from the CQDs [57]. Upon PANI modification (Fig. 5h), an increased intensity in the C–N peak confirms successful integration of nitrogen-rich PANI onto the composite surface [58]. The N 1 s spectrum further elucidates the types of nitrogen functionalities present. In both samples (Fig. 5d and i), deconvoluted peaks at ∼ 398.9 eV (pyridinic N), ∼400.6 eV (graphitic N), and ∼ 401.2 eV (pyrrolic N/protonated amine) are observed. Notably, the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI composite shows an enhanced pyrrolic/amine contribution, implying a higher density of protonated nitrogen groups, which serve as favorable coordination sites for Cd2+ ions [59–61]. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 5e and j) reveal peaks at ∼ 529.9 eV (Fe–O lattice), ∼531.4 eV (Fe–OH/C–O), and ∼ 533.0 eV (CO/O–H), indicating the coexistence of metal–oxygen bonds and surface hydroxyl/carbonyl groups. These oxygen-containing groups, especially surface –OH, can act as secondary binding sites for metal ions. The synergistic effect of pyridinic/pyrrolic nitrogen and oxygenated functional groups enhances the chelation and selective interaction with Cd2+ ions. Nitrogen atoms especially pyridinic N act as electron-rich Lewis base sites, coordinating with electron-deficient Cd2+ through lone pair donation [54,62,63]. Additionally, the incorporation of PANI increases the number of active nitrogen sites, further boosting the binding affinity and sensor response.

Together, the XPS results confirm that nitrogen doping and PANI functionalization introduce abundant and diverse active sites (N- and O-based), which play a pivotal role in selective Cd2+ sensing via strong coordination interactions. The XPS results after Cd2+ adsorption for the ternary nanocomposite of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI was provided in Supplementary information.

Electrochemical sensing is a promising method for detecting heavy metal ions in water. This technique is fast, portable, and cost effective. It is performed using a three-electrode system, the working, reference, and counter electrodes. The presence of heavy metal ions changes the current, potential, or other electrochemical properties of the working electrode. These changes will then be converted into electrical signals, which can be used to detect the heavy metal ions. The N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite working electrode is subjected to electrochemical sensing of heavy metal ions for environmental applications. Fig. 6 a shows the comparative CV profiles of N-CQDs, N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3, and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrodes in the presence of 1 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite electrode shows a higher peak current value indicating better conductivity as a result of efficient electron transfer ability. A pair of well-defined redox peaks were observed for N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite with a peak separation of 89 mV. Further, the unary and binary nanocomposite shows a peak separation of 146 and 95 mV, respectively which shows a higher peak-to-peak shift than the ternary nanocomposite. The higher conductivity observed in the ternary nanocomposite well agrees with the EIS measurements.

As depicted in Fig. 6b, the EIS curves of the N-CQDs and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite indicate higher resistance compared to the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode. The higher resistance could be attributed to the negative ions present in the redox probe, which potentially impede electron transport within the unary and binary nanocomposite. Notably, the Rct values were determined to be around 410 Ω cm−2 for the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode and 1321 Ω cm−2, 731 Ω cm−2 for the N-CQDs and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite. This low Rct for the modified electrode is in line with the CV plot of the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode. Hence, the different scan rate studies were taken for N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode (Fig. 6c) to determine the linearity and process involved in the redox process ranging from 10 mVs−1. Here, we can see the linear increase of peak currents with the scan rate increment. This confirms the controlled diffusion process involved in the whole reaction and the linearity of the redox process. The linear regression relation obtained from the scan rate is given in Fig. 6d. The equations for redox couple for oxidation and reduction with regression coefficients. It further confirms that the ternary nanocomposite undergoes a two-electron transfer reaction in the electrocatalysis reversible process.
The equation for oxidation → y = 2.8829x + 19.237; R2 = 0.9833
The equation for reduction → y = -2.0617x − 18.937; R2 = 0.977

Through a series of pH experiments, the optimal pH for detecting Cd2+ (500 µM) and electrochemical behaviour of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite was established in Fig. 7 a. These experiments involved testing the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite in conjunction with PBS buffer solutions spanning a pH range from 3 to 11 using SWV. Peak potential moves from the higher to the lower end when pH values rise. The greater peak current observed in pH 5 is caused by the efficient binding of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite with the –OH groups of the target analyte in an acidic pH, which is caused by an increase in H+ ions. The peak current falls when pH rises because of an increase in –OH groups in the buffer solution brought on by the analyte and nanocomposites repulsive force. This explains the potential shift towards a lower value and the full disappearance of the peak current above pH 5. Additionally, at a pH 5, the oxidation of the Cd2+ ions produces a peak at 1.0 µA. The findings revealed that the peak current exhibited its best performance at pH 5. Fig. 7b displays the sensing ability of the prepared N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite. It was assessed through an experiment comparing the performance with N-CQDs and N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite in 10 mL of PBS containing 500 µM Cd2+ ions at pH 5. It reveals that the electrocatalytic activity towards Cd2+ sensing in ternary nanocomposite modified electrodes shows better performance.

The SWV was employed in 10 mL of PBS at the analyte Cd2+ concentrations ranging from 1 µM to 1 mM using N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode in Fig. 8 a with the standard addition method. The results demonstrate a linear increase in the peak current as the concentration of Cd2+ is raised with the correlation coefficient of 0.9921 (Fig. 8b) and the detection limit was found to be 750 nM. A comparison of the electrochemical detection of various analytes for different combinations of nanomaterials with the current work was shown in Table 1 . The table exhibit that the combination of the modified electrode exhibits the sensing performance towards Cd2+ ions compared to the individual components and other specific combinations of the synthesized electrode material. Further, it is proposed that the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite modified electrode demonstrates the overall performance for the detection of Cd2+ ions by combining the strengths of its components while overcoming their individual limitations. This pioneering approach positions it as a promising candidate for future applications in environmental monitoring and sensing technologies.

To determine the selectivity of the material towards interferents, an experiment was carried out in a solution containing 500 μM cadmium along with other interferents, such as Co2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, K+, Na2+, and Zn2+. It confirms that the prepared nanocomposite is highly selective for the Cd2+ ions even on the existence of other metal ions. These results (Fig. 9 a) confirmed that the addition of interfering species did not affect the cadmium detection. From this, it is observable that the proposed modified electrode is selective. The stability of the modified electrode was investigated by performing repeated CV (100 cycles at 50 mV s−1) in 500 μM of Cd2+ (Fig. 9b). It recommends that the modified electrode has good stability and maintains its electrochemical activity in the presence of Cd2+ ions. Further, it could be considered for long term use in sensing applications particularly for intensive care heavy metals in innumerable environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ ions in a spiked water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were collected from designated locations within the university campus and immediately stored in pre-cleaned polypropylene containers at 6 °C to minimize contamination and preserve sample integrity. Prior to analysis, the samples were subjected to centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 10 mins. The supernatant was subsequently filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane syringe filter to eliminate residual fine particulates. Recovery studies were conducted by spiking the filtered water samples with known concentrations of Cd2+ standard solutions.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the fabricated sensor for Cd2+ detection, recovery experiments were conducted using spiked tap water and commercial drinking water samples. The developed N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI/GCE electrochemical sensor was employed under optimized experimental conditions for the quantification of Cd2+. The results, summarized in Table 2 , demonstrated satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 95 % to 99 % for both tap water and drinking water. The corresponding RSD (%) values were found to be within 2–4 %, indicating good precision and reproducibility. These findings confirm the sensor’s excellent potential for reliable Cd2+ detection in real water matrices. The plot for the real sample analysis of tap water and drinking water using the proposed sensor was provided in the supplementary as Figs. S2 and S3.

The vital need for rapid and sensitive detection of heavy-metal ions in natural water environments arises from their serious threats to human health and ecosystems. We have successfully synthesized a nanocomposite composed of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite and evaluated its electrochemical performance for detecting heavy metal ions. This innovative approach promises to enhance the accuracy and speed of heavy metal ion monitoring, contributing to improved environmental safety and public health protection. The findings reveal utility for the N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite in environmental sensing, particularly for Cd2+ ion detection. The modified electrode displays outstanding sensitivity and a wide linear range for detecting Cd2+ ions, with a low detection limit of 750 nM. This research underscores the significance of N-CQDs@α-Fe2O3/PANI nanocomposite interactions in creating efficient electrochemical sensors establishing the nanocomposite as a superior option in this field. This functionality underscores the versatility and the significance of our research findings in addressing environmental challenges.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: S. Abinaya is currently a Research Scholar in the School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Tamil Nadu, India. She obtained her BSc in Physics from Sri Adi Chunchanagiri Women’s College, Cumbum and subsequently earned her M.Sc in Physics from Madurai Kamaraj University. She also served as a RUSA Project Assistant in the Advanced Material Laboratory at the same institution, where she focused on developing novel fluorescent detectors for the effective identification of toxic heavy metal ions. Her research interests lie at the intersection of advanced materials for energy storage and environmental applications. She has gained extensive hands-on experience with electrochemical techniques, coin cell fabrication, and materials characterization. She has presented her findings at several national and international conferences and has published in reputable journals her strong commitment to multidisciplinary research and continuous learning drives her pursuit of innovative solutions for energy and environmental challenges.

Dr. S. Girija received her B. Sc (2011–2014), M.Sc. (2014–2016) and M.Phil. (2017–2019) degrees in School of Physics from Madurai Kamaraj University, Tamil Nadu, India. She served as Project fellow in Alagappa University, Karaikudi, India during May 2019 to Jan 2020 in the areas of biosensors. She pursued her Ph.D. in the Department of Bioelectronics and Biosensors, Alagappa University. Her research activity is focused on the synthesis of MOFs/ZIFs for sensor applications. Currently she is a research intern at Kaohsiung Medical University at Kaohsiung, Taiwan.

Dr. Anjana Baby received her Ph.D. in Chemistry from CHRIST (Deemed to be University), India. Her research primarily focuses on the synthesis and functionalization of two-dimensional (2D) materials. She has worked extensively on developing advanced materials for electrochemical energy storage devices, including supercapacitors and batteries. In addition, her work explores the design of solar thermal fuels for efficient energy conversion and storage. She possesses strong expertise in material characterization techniques and device fabrication processes. Currently, she is a postdoctoral researcher at the Jožef Stefan Institute, Slovenia, where she continues to contribute to advanced research in energy-related materials.

Dr. A. Aseema Banu completed her Ph.D. in the School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai in 2021, and she received her M. Sc and M. Phil degree in Physics from the same university. Her research interest is Computational Materials Physics that includes materials modelling for energy, environment, and biomedical applications. She also served as a Associate professor in Sethu institute of technology, Virudhunagar, Tamil Nadu. She has been working extensively in materials design for gas sensors, hydrogen storage, battery, and supercapacitors. She had done most part of her research work in the Department of Science and Technology Materials for Energy Storage (DST-MES) Major research programme (DST/TMD/MES/2 K1 7/94(G)), New Delhi, India for computational support. She has always been committed in completing a task within the stipulated time. Her Ph. D was on computational studies of the structural, electronic and surface properties of pure and doped metal carbides for energy storage, catalysis, and gas sensors from the first-principles calculations.

Dr. Sujin P. Jose received her Ph.D. in 2006 from Manonmaniam Sundaranar University, Tamil Nadu. She is currently an Assistant Professor and Head of the Department of Computational Physics at the School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, India. She has also served as a Visiting Professor at the Alternative Energy Materials Laboratory at Arizona State University, USA, and a Visiting Scientist in the Department of Materials Science and NanoEngineering at Rice University, USA. Her research focuses on nanostructured materials for energy storage, including supercapacitors, batteries, and fuel cells and the design of nanocomposites for healthcare and environmental remediation as well as quantum chemical investigations of polyatomic molecules. Dr. Jose has published extensively in high-impact journals, contributed to multiple book chapters, and led collaborative research projects supported by national and international funding agencies. In recognition of her contributions to advanced materials research, she was honoured as an International Distinguished Researcher in Energy Storage (2020).
